charts.title: Week 11 Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of all major topics covered in Week 11: multiwinner elections, committee scoring rules, Thiele methods, and proportional representation.

nodes.label:
Week 11
Multiwinner Elections
nodes.note: Multiwinner elections select a committee of k candidates rather than a single winner. The right rule depends on the goal: excellence (best individuals), diversity (broad coverage), or proportional representation (reflect voter groups).

nodes.label:
Types of
Multiwinner Rules
nodes.note: Three distinct goals drive multiwinner rule design: excellence selects the best k individuals independently, diversity ensures different perspectives are represented, and proportional representation reflects voter group sizes in the committee.

nodes.label:
Single Transferable
Vote (STV)
nodes.note: STV generalises IRV to committee elections using a quota of floor(n/(k+1))+1. Candidates meeting the quota are elected and surplus votes transferred; the weakest candidate is eliminated if no one meets the quota.

nodes.label:
Committee
Scoring Rules
nodes.note: Committee scoring rules extend positional scoring rules to committees. A scoring function assigns a value to each possible position vector (where committee members appear in the ranking), and the winning committee maximises total score.

nodes.label:
Thiele Methods
(Approval-Based)
nodes.note: Thiele methods work with approval ballots. A weight vector determines the marginal utility of each additional approved committee member: AV uses equal weights (excellence), CC uses (1,0,...) (diversity), PAV uses harmonic weights (proportionality).

nodes.label:
Justified
Representation
nodes.note: Justified representation (JR) and its stronger variant EJR guarantee that cohesive voter groups of sufficient size receive proportional representation in the committee. PAV satisfies EJR; CC satisfies JR; AV satisfies neither.

nodes.label:
Participatory
Budgeting
nodes.note: Participatory budgeting selects a subset of projects under a cost budget. The greedy method iteratively picks the most-approved affordable project, approximating the solution to a knapsack problem.

nodes.label: SNTV
nodes.note: Single Non-Transferable Vote scores a committee by counting voters whose first choice is in the committee. Only the best-positioned committee member matters for each voter.

nodes.label: Bloc
nodes.note: Bloc scoring counts appearances in each voter's top-k preferences. Each voter effectively casts k votes, and the k most-mentioned candidates win, similar to plurality applied to committees.

nodes.label: k-Borda
nodes.note: k-Borda selects the k candidates with the highest individual Borda scores. It is an excellence-based rule that rewards candidates who are broadly preferred across all voters' rankings.

nodes.label:
Chamberlin-Courant
(CC)
nodes.note: Chamberlin-Courant scores a committee by summing each voter's Borda score for their best representative in the committee. It captures diversity by rewarding committees that include at least one candidate each voter likes.

nodes.label:
Approval Voting
(AV)
nodes.note: Approval Voting selects the k candidates with the most approvals. Each additional approved candidate contributes equally, so it maximises total welfare but ignores diminishing returns and proportionality.

nodes.label:
PAV
(Harmonic Weights)
nodes.note: Proportional Approval Voting uses harmonic weights (1, 1/2, 1/3, ...) to give diminishing marginal utility for each additional approved representative. It is the unique Thiele method satisfying EJR.

charts.title: Single Transferable Vote (STV) Algorithm
charts.description: Step-by-step procedure for electing a committee of k candidates using the Single Transferable Vote method.

nodes.label:
Start: n voters,
m candidates, size k
nodes.note: We have n voters with ranked ballots over m candidates and want to elect a committee of k candidates.

nodes.label:
Compute quota
q = floor(n/(k+1)) + 1
nodes.note: The Droop quota q is the minimum number of first-place votes needed to guarantee election. With k=1, this reduces to a majority quota, making STV equivalent to IRV.

nodes.label:
Elected = 0,
all candidates active
nodes.note: Initialise the set of elected candidates as empty and all candidates as active. Voter ballots will be updated as candidates are elected or eliminated.

nodes.label:
Elected k
candidates?
nodes.note: Check whether we have already selected exactly k candidates. If so, the committee is complete and we are done.

nodes.label:
COMMITTEE
ELECTED
nodes.note: The elected set contains exactly k candidates, forming the winning committee under STV.

nodes.label:
Count first-place
votes per candidate
nodes.note: For each active candidate, count how many voters currently have them ranked first on their active ballot.

nodes.label:
Any candidate
reaches quota q?
nodes.note: Check whether any active candidate has at least q first-place votes. Meeting the quota triggers election and surplus transfer.

nodes.label:
Elect that
candidate c
nodes.note: Add c to the elected set. If c has more than q votes (a surplus of votes-q), transfer q of their supporters' ballots to those voters' next-ranked active candidate.

nodes.label:
Remove c from
active candidates
nodes.note: Remove the elected candidate c from all remaining active ballots so future rounds only consider unelected candidates.

nodes.label:
Eliminate candidate
with fewest votes
nodes.note: No candidate met the quota, so eliminate the active candidate with the fewest first-place votes (use tie-breaking if needed). Their supporters' votes transfer to the next active candidate on those ballots.

charts.title: Choosing a Thiele Method
charts.description: Decision guide for selecting the appropriate Thiele approval-based voting method based on the desired committee goal.

nodes.label:
Start: approval
ballots, committee size k
nodes.note: We have approval ballots from n voters and want to elect a committee of k candidates. The right Thiele method depends on what the committee should optimise.

nodes.label:
What is the
committee goal?
nodes.note: Three distinct goals lead to different weight vectors. Excellence: select the best-liked individuals. Diversity: ensure broad coverage. Proportional: reflect voter group sizes.

nodes.label:
Excellence goal:
maximise total approvals
nodes.note: Use Approval Voting (AV). Each approved committee member contributes equally to total welfare. Weight vector is (1, 1, 1, ...). Maximises the sum of utility across all voters.

nodes.label:
Diversity goal:
maximise voter coverage
nodes.note: Use Chamberlin-Courant (CC). Only the first approved representative counts for each voter. Weight vector is (1, 0, 0, ...). Maximises the number of voters who have at least one representative.

nodes.label:
Proportional goal:
reflect group sizes
nodes.note: Use Proportional Approval Voting (PAV). Harmonic weight vector (1, 1/2, 1/3, ...) gives diminishing returns for additional representatives. The unique Thiele method satisfying extended justified representation (EJR).

nodes.label:
USE: Approval
Voting (AV)
nodes.note: AV selects the k candidates with the most total approvals. It is computationally efficient (polynomial) but can systematically exclude minority voter groups.

nodes.label:
USE: Chamberlin-
Courant (CC)
nodes.note: CC maximises the number of voters with at least one approved representative in the committee. Computing the optimal CC committee is NP-hard; greedy approximations are used in practice.

nodes.label:
USE: Proportional
Approval Voting (PAV)
nodes.note: PAV balances excellence and diversity via harmonic diminishing returns. It satisfies EJR, guaranteeing proportional representation for cohesive voter groups. Computing PAV is NP-hard.

charts.title: Checking Justified Representation (JR / EJR)
charts.description: Procedure to verify whether a given committee satisfies Justified Representation or Extended Justified Representation for an approval ballot election.

nodes.label:
Start: committee W,
approval ballots, k, n
nodes.note: We have a candidate committee W, approval ballots from n voters, committee size k, and want to check whether W satisfies JR or EJR.

nodes.label:
Set ell = 1
(start with JR)
nodes.note: JR corresponds to ell=1. EJR also requires ell=2,3,...,k. We check each level ell in turn.

nodes.label:
Find group S with
|S| >= ell*n/k
nodes.note: Search for a group S of voters with at least ell*n/k members who all share at least ell commonly approved candidates (the intersection of their approval sets has size at least ell).

nodes.label:
Does such a
cohesive group exist?
nodes.note: Check whether any group S of sufficient size exists with at least ell candidates approved by all members. If no such group exists, the condition is vacuously satisfied for this ell.

nodes.label:
Does any voter in S
have utility >= ell?
nodes.note: Check whether at least one voter i in S has at least ell approved candidates in committee W (i.e. |A_i intersection W| >= ell). If yes, the group is proportionally represented.

nodes.label:
VIOLATION: JR/EJR
NOT SATISFIED
nodes.note: A cohesive group of sufficient size exists but no member has enough representatives in W. The committee violates JR (if ell=1) or EJR (if ell>1). The rule that selected W may not satisfy proportional representation.

nodes.label: ell = ell + 1
nodes.note: The condition is satisfied for this level ell. Move to the next level to check the stronger EJR condition.

nodes.label:
Checked all
ell up to k?
nodes.note: EJR requires checking all levels from ell=1 to ell=k. If all levels pass, the committee satisfies EJR (which implies JR).

nodes.label:
EJR SATISFIED
(implies JR too)
nodes.note: All ell-levels have been checked and no violation was found. The committee W satisfies Extended Justified Representation, guaranteeing proportional representation for all cohesive voter groups.
